faqs.question: What technical resources are available for Cincon products?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive technical resources for Cincon products including: Datasheets with detailed electrical and mechanical specifications; Application notes covering design guidelines, PCB layout recommendations, and thermal management; Selection guides to help choose the right product for your application; White papers on power supply design topics; CAD models and footprints for PCB design; Certification files including safety certificates and test reports; MTBF calculations and reliability data; Sample code and reference designs where applicable. These resources are available for download from our website or by contacting our technical support team. As an authorized Cincon distributor, we have access to the complete technical documentation library and can provide additional support for specific application questions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our technical library or contact support for specific documentation requests.

To request samples: Contact our sales team with part numbers and quantities; Provide project information and expected annual usage; Specify application and requirements; Samples shipped with datasheets and application notes. For production programs: Sample evaluation support from our FAE team; Design review and recommendations; Volume pricing and scheduling discussions. Contact our sales team to request samples or discuss your evaluation needs.

Support process: Initial contact to understand your question; Research and analysis by our FAE team; Detailed response with recommendations; Follow-up as needed to resolve the issue. articles.title: Cincon DC-DC Converter Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting the right Cincon DC-DC converter for your application based on power requirements, input range, isolation needs, and environmental conditions.
articles.content: Selecting the right DC-DC converter is critical for reliable power system design. This guide walks through the key selection criteria for Cincon DC-DC converters. Power Requirements: Determine your output power needs including headroom for future expansion. Cincon offers converters from 1W to 60W. Consider operating at 50-70% of rated power for improved reliability and thermal performance. Input Voltage Range: Identify your input voltage source and range. Use 2:1 converters (e.g., 9-18V, 18-36V) for stable sources. Use 4:1 converters (e.g., 9-36V) for battery or variable sources. Ensure adequate margin for voltage transients. Output Voltage: Select standard output voltages (3.3V, 5V, 9V, 12V, 15V, 24V) based on your load requirements. Consider voltage drop in cables for remote loads. Isolation Requirements: Basic industrial: 1000VDC isolation sufficient. Enhanced safety: 1500VDC to 3000VDC. Medical applications: 5000VDC with 2xMOPP. Package Selection: SIP for compact designs and automated assembly. DIP for manual assembly and prototyping. SMD for high-density PCBs. Chassis mount for higher power. Environmental Considerations: Temperature range: Standard -40°C to +85°C. Humidity and contamination protection. Vibration and shock requirements. Altitude derating if applicable. Certifications: UL, CE, CB for general applications. IEC 60601-1 for medical. EN 50155 for railway. Contact our FAE team for personalized selection assistance.

faqs.question: How do I determine the right power rating for my application?
faqs.answer: To determine the appropriate power rating: Calculate maximum load power: Sum the power requirements of all loads; Include peak power demands if applicable; Add 20-30% headroom for reliability. Consider derating: Operating at 50-70% of rated power improves reliability; Reduces temperature rise; Extends converter lifetime. Account for variations: Input voltage variations affect output capability; Temperature affects maximum power; Aging may reduce capacity over time. Example calculation: Load power: 3W maximum; Headroom (30%): 0.9W; Total required: 3.9W; Select 4W or 6W converter (EC4A or EC6A series). For battery applications, consider efficiency impact on battery life. Higher efficiency converters reduce power consumption. Contact our FAE team for power budgeting assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate load power plus 20-30% headroom. Contact us for power budgeting assistance.

faqs.question: When should I choose 2:1 vs 4:1 input range converters?
faqs.answer: Choose input range based on your power source characteristics: 2:1 Input Range (e.g., 9-18V, 18-36V, 36-72V): Best for: Stable regulated power sources; Fixed battery systems with narrow voltage range; Applications where efficiency is critical; Cost-sensitive designs. Advantages: Slightly higher efficiency (1-2%); Lower cost; Simpler internal design. 4:1 Input Range (e.g., 9-36V, 18-75V): Best for: Battery systems with wide discharge range; Applications with multiple input sources; Systems with significant voltage transients; Universal input requirements. Advantages: Handles wider voltage variations; Single converter for multiple battery types; Better transient immunity. Decision matrix: Stable 24V industrial: 18-36V (2:1). 12V lead-acid battery: 9-18V (2:1) or 9-36V (4:1). 24V battery with transients: 9-36V (4:1). Multiple battery types: 4:1 for flexibility. Contact our FAE team for input range selection guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 2:1 for stable sources, 4:1 for batteries or variable inputs. Contact us for selection guidance.

faqs.question: What isolation voltage do I need for my application?
faqs.answer: Isolation voltage requirements depend on safety standards and application: Functional Isolation (1000VDC): Basic circuit separation; No safety certification required; Suitable for internal subsystems; Cost-effective option. Basic Isolation (1500V-3000VDC): Operator safety protection; Meets IEC 60950/62368 for IT equipment; Suitable for industrial controls; Provides noise immunity. Reinforced Isolation (3000V-5000VDC): High safety applications; Medical equipment (MOOP); Harsh industrial environments; Enhanced protection. Medical 2xMOPP (5000VAC): Patient contact applications; IEC 60601-1 certification required; Ultra-low leakage current (<2µA); Highest safety level. Selection guidelines: Industrial control: 1000V-1500VDC sufficient. Telecom/datacom: 1500V-2000VDC typical. Medical (no patient contact): 3000VDC (MOOP). Medical (patient contact): 5000VAC (2xMOPP). Railway: 3000VDC typical. When in doubt, specify higher isolation. Contact our FAE team for isolation requirements analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match isolation to safety standards for your application. Contact us for requirements analysis.

faqs.question: How do I select the right package type?
faqs.answer: Package selection depends on assembly process and space constraints: SIP (Single In-line Package): Best for: Space-constrained designs; Automated through-hole assembly; Low to medium power (1-6W). Advantages: Most compact through-hole; Good for high-density PCBs; Cost-effective. DIP (Dual In-line Package): Best for: Manual assembly and prototyping; Applications requiring mechanical stability; Medium power (2-20W). Advantages: Easier manual soldering; Better mechanical retention; Wider pin spacing. SMD (Surface Mount Device): Best for: Automated SMT assembly; High-volume production; Space-constrained designs. Advantages: Smallest footprint; Automated placement; High-density integration. Chassis Mount: Best for: Higher power applications (10-60W); Heat sinking requirements; Panel mounting. Advantages: Excellent thermal performance; Robust mounting; Easy field replacement. Selection factors: Assembly process (manual vs automated); Available PCB space; Thermal requirements; Mechanical stability needs; Cost considerations. Contact our FAE team for package recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: SIP for compact automated, DIP for manual, SMD for SMT, chassis for high power. Contact us for recommendations.

faqs.question: What temperature rating do I need for my application?
faqs.answer: Temperature rating selection depends on operating environment: Standard Commercial (0°C to +70°C): Office and indoor environments; Consumer electronics; Cost-sensitive applications; Limited availability in Cincon portfolio. Industrial Extended (-40°C to +85°C): Factory automation; Outdoor equipment; Transportation; Most Cincon converters rated to this range. High Temperature (-40°C to +105°C or higher): Severe industrial environments; Engine compartments; Specialized applications; May require derating or special models. Selection considerations: Ambient temperature: Measure or estimate maximum expected; Include margin for hot spots and enclosures. Self-heating: Calculate temperature rise from power dissipation; Add to ambient for total temperature. Derating: Many converters require power derating above 70°C or 85°C; Check datasheet derating curves. Reliability: Every 10°C increase halves lifetime; Conservative temperature improves reliability. Most industrial applications use -40°C to +85°C rated converters. Contact our FAE team for high-temperature application support.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use -40°C to +85°C for most industrial. Consider derating for high ambient. Contact us for high-temp support.

customerCases.feedback: Guide was very helpful for our design

articles.title: PCB Layout Guidelines for Cincon Converters
articles.summary: Best practices for PCB layout when using Cincon DC-DC converters including component placement, grounding, thermal management, and EMI considerations.
articles.content: Proper PCB layout is essential for optimal performance and reliability of DC-DC converters. This application note provides guidelines for laying out PCBs with Cincon converters. Input Capacitor Placement: Place input capacitor as close as possible to converter input pins (within 5mm); Use short, wide traces to minimize inductance; Connect capacitor ground directly to input ground plane; Use ceramic capacitors (X5R or X7R) for best performance; Typical value: 4.7-10µF for low power, higher for high power. Output Capacitor Placement: Place output capacitor close to output pins (within 5mm); Minimize trace length between converter and capacitor; Connect to output ground plane; Use ceramic or low-ESR electrolytic; Typical value: 4.7-10µF, adjust for ripple requirements. Grounding: Use solid ground planes for both input and output; Connect planes at single point near converter if isolated; Minimize ground loop area; Keep high-frequency currents localized. Thermal Management: Provide copper area for heat dissipation; Use thermal vias under converter if applicable; Consider heatsink for high power or high ambient; Maintain adequate spacing from heat-sensitive components. EMI Considerations: Minimize input and output loop areas; Keep high-frequency switching nodes away from sensitive circuits; Use ground planes for shielding; Consider shielding cans for sensitive applications. Layout Review Checklist: Capacitors placed within 5mm of pins; Adequate copper for current capacity; Proper grounding implemented; Thermal management addressed; Clearance/creepage for isolation voltage. Contact our FAE team for layout review services.

faqs.question: How close should input and output capacitors be placed to the converter?
faqs.answer: Input and output capacitors should be placed as close as possible to the converter pins: Recommended distance: Within 5mm (0.2 inches) of the pins; Closer is better for high-frequency performance; Maximum 10mm for acceptable performance. Trace considerations: Use short, wide traces between capacitor and pins; Minimize inductance in the loop; Width: minimum 0.5mm per amp of current; Use multiple vias for connections to planes. Why close placement matters: Reduces parasitic inductance; Improves filtering effectiveness; Minimizes EMI radiation; Ensures stable operation. Practical layout tips: Place capacitors on same side as converter if possible; Orient capacitors to minimize trace length; Use copper pours for low-impedance connections; Keep capacitor ground close to pin ground. For high-frequency noise reduction, add small (0.1µF) ceramic capacitors even closer to the pins. Contact our FAE team for specific layout recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Place capacitors within 5mm of pins. Use short, wide traces. Contact us for layout review.

faqs.question: What grounding approach should I use for isolated converters?
faqs.answer: Isolated converters require careful grounding to maintain isolation and minimize noise: Single-point grounding: Connect input and output grounds at one point only; Typically near the converter; Prevents ground loops; Maintains isolation barrier integrity. Ground plane strategy: Use separate ground planes for input and output sides; Connect planes at the single point; Minimize coupling between planes. Layout guidelines: Route all input circuits on input ground plane; Route all output circuits on output ground plane; Do not cross isolation barrier with traces or copper; Maintain creepage and clearance distances. Noise reduction: Keep high-frequency currents localized; Minimize ground loop area on each side; Use ground planes for shielding; Consider split planes for sensitive circuits. For systems with multiple converters: Each converter maintains its own isolation; Connect input grounds together; Connect output grounds based on system requirements; May need multiple ground points for complex systems. Medical applications: Keep patient ground separate from chassis ground; Follow IEC 60601-1 grounding requirements; Document grounding in risk management file. Contact our FAE team for grounding design assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use single-point grounding. Keep input/output planes separate. Contact us for grounding design.

faqs.question: How do I manage thermal dissipation on the PCB?
faqs.answer: Thermal management is critical for reliable converter operation: Heat sources: Converter power dissipation: Pd = Pout × (1/η - 1); Example: 10W output at 85% efficiency = 1.76W dissipation. PCB thermal design: Copper area: Provide adequate copper for heat spreading; Use 1oz or 2oz copper for better conduction; Thermal vias: Place under converter if package allows; Use multiple vias (0.3mm diameter typical); Connect to internal ground planes. Layout considerations: Keep converter away from heat-sensitive components; Allow space for airflow if convection cooled; Consider thermal shadowing from tall components. Thermal calculations: Estimate temperature rise: ΔT = Pd × θja; θja depends on package and copper area; Check datasheet thermal curves. Improvement techniques: Increase copper area (diminishing returns above ~10cm²); Add thermal vias to inner planes; Use heatsink for high power; Implement forced airflow. Measurement: Measure case temperature with thermocouple; Verify below maximum rating; Allow margin for worst-case conditions. For high-temperature applications, contact our FAE team for thermal analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide copper area and thermal vias. Measure temperature in operation. Contact us for thermal analysis.

faqs.question: What trace widths should I use for power connections?
faqs.answer: Power trace sizing depends on current and temperature rise: Current capacity: General rule: 0.5mm (20 mil) width per amp for 1oz copper; Increase for higher current or lower temperature rise; Use online calculators for precise sizing. Temperature rise: Standard designs: 10°C rise acceptable; High-reliability: 5°C rise preferred; Consider ambient temperature in calculation. Trace width guidelines: Input traces: Size for maximum input current at minimum voltage; Example: 10W at 9V = 1.4A minimum, use 1mm+ trace. Output traces: Size for full load current; Example: 5V at 2A = 2A, use 1mm+ trace. Copper thickness: 1oz (35µm) copper standard; 2oz (70µm) for higher current; Reduces resistance and heating. Practical recommendations: Use copper pours for high-current paths; Multiple vias for layer transitions; Keep traces short to minimize resistance; Consider voltage drop in long traces. For high-current applications: Use multiple parallel traces; Implement copper pours instead of traces; Consider external bus bars for very high current. Contact our FAE team for specific trace sizing recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 0.5mm per amp minimum. Consider temperature rise. Contact us for specific recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I minimize EMI in my converter layout?
faqs.answer: EMI minimization requires attention to layout details: Input loop minimization: Place input capacitor very close to pins; Minimize loop area formed by input traces; Use wide, short traces; This reduces conducted EMI. Output loop minimization: Place output capacitor close to pins; Minimize output loop area; Keep output traces away from input. Ground plane strategy: Use continuous ground planes for shielding; Minimize slots and cuts in planes; Connect planes with multiple vias. Component placement: Keep converter away from sensitive circuits; Orient converter to minimize coupling; Use distance for isolation. Filtering: Add input filter if required for compliance; Place filter components close to converter; Use shielded inductors if applicable. Layout techniques: Use guard rings around sensitive circuits; Implement moats and bridges for isolation; Keep high-frequency nodes away from edges; Consider internal layers for sensitive traces. Shielding: Use ground planes on multiple layers; Consider shielding cans for severe cases; Metal enclosures provide additional shielding. Testing: Perform pre-compliance testing early; Identify and address issues before final design; Allow margin for production variation. Contact our FAE team for EMI design review.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize loop areas, use ground planes, keep away from sensitive circuits. Contact us for EMI review.

customerCases.feedback: Guide was very helpful for our design

articles.title: Thermal Management for Cincon Power Converters
articles.summary: Guidelines for thermal management of Cincon DC-DC and AC-DC converters including heat sinking, airflow, and derating considerations.
articles.content: Thermal management is essential for reliable power converter operation. This guide covers thermal design for Cincon converters. Understanding Power Dissipation: Calculate dissipation: Pd = Pout × (1/η - 1); Example: 20W output at 85% efficiency = 3.53W dissipated as heat. This heat must be removed to prevent overheating. Thermal Ratings: Operating temperature range: -40°C to +85°C typical; Maximum junction/case temperature: typically 105°C to 125°C; Derating may be required at high ambient temperatures. Natural Convection Cooling: Suitable for: Low power applications (<5W); Moderate ambient temperatures (<50°C); Adequate clearance around converter. Requirements: Minimum 10mm clearance on all sides; Vertical mounting preferred for airflow; Avoid obstructing air paths. Forced Air Cooling: Benefits: Extends temperature capability; Allows higher power density; Improves reliability at high ambient. Implementation: Add fan or use system airflow; 200-400 LFM typical for significant improvement; Ensure airflow reaches converter. Conduction Cooling: Used for: Encapsulated modules; High-power applications; Sealed enclosures. Method: Mount converter on metal surface or heatsink; Use thermal interface material; Connect to chassis for heat spreading. Heatsink Selection: Considerations: Thermal resistance (°C/W); Size and mounting; Cost and availability. Calculation: Determine required thermal resistance; Select heatsink with adequate margin; Consider airflow if using forced convection. Derating Curves: Most converters require power derating above 70-85°C ambient; Check datasheet curves for specific product; Plan for worst-case operating conditions. Thermal Testing: Measure case temperature with thermocouple; Verify below maximum rating; Test at worst-case conditions (max load, min airflow, max ambient). Contact our FAE team for thermal analysis assistance.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the heatsink size needed for my application?
faqs.answer: Heatsink sizing requires thermal calculations: Determine heat load: Calculate converter dissipation: Pd = Pout × (1/η - 1); Example: 15W output at 88% efficiency = 2.05W. Determine temperature limits: Maximum allowed case temperature: Tc_max (typically 105°C); Maximum ambient temperature: Ta_max (your application); Allowable temperature rise: ΔT = Tc_max - Ta_max. Calculate required thermal resistance: θsa = ΔT / Pd - θjc - θcs; Where: θjc = junction-to-case thermal resistance; θcs = case-to-sink thermal resistance (interface); θsa = sink-to-ambient thermal resistance (heatsink). Example calculation: Tc_max = 105°C, Ta_max = 70°C, Pd = 2W; ΔT = 35°C; θjc = 15°C/W, θcs = 1°C/W (with grease); θsa = 35/2 - 15 - 1 = 1.5°C/W. Select heatsink: Find heatsink with θsa ≤ calculated value; Consider natural convection vs forced air; Add margin for safety (20% typical). Contact our FAE team for heatsink selection assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required thermal resistance and select heatsink accordingly. Contact us for assistance.

faqs.question: What airflow is required for forced air cooling?
faqs.answer: Airflow requirements depend on power and temperature: Natural convection baseline: No airflow required; Limited to lower power or moderate ambient; Typical limit: 50-60°C ambient at full power. Forced air benefits: Extends operating temperature range; Allows higher power density; Improves reliability. Airflow guidelines: 100 LFM (Linear Feet per Minute): Moderate improvement; 200 LFM: Significant improvement for most applications; 400+ LFM: For high power or severe conditions. Measurement and calculation: LFM = CFM / Area (in square feet); Example: 10 CFM through 0.1 sq ft = 100 LFM. Implementation considerations: Ensure airflow reaches converter; Avoid hot air recirculation; Filter air to prevent contamination; Consider fan noise and reliability. System airflow: Use existing system fans if available; Position converter in airflow path; Ensure adequate inlet and exhaust. Testing: Measure temperature with airflow; Verify adequate cooling; Test at worst-case conditions. For most applications, 200 LFM provides good improvement over natural convection. Contact our FAE team for airflow optimization.
faqs.decisionGuide: 200 LFM typical for good improvement. Ensure airflow reaches converter. Contact us for optimization.

faqs.question: How do I use derating curves for high-temperature operation?
faqs.answer: Derating curves show maximum power vs ambient temperature: Understanding curves: X-axis: Ambient temperature (°C); Y-axis: Maximum output power (% of rated); Curve shows allowable power at each temperature. Typical derating: Full power to 70-85°C ambient; Linear derating above that temperature; Zero power at maximum rated temperature. Using the curves: Find your maximum ambient temperature on X-axis; Read allowable power percentage on Y-axis; Multiply by rated power for maximum allowed. Example: 10W converter, 70°C ambient; Curve shows 80% at 70°C; Maximum power = 10W × 0.8 = 8W. Design margin: Operate below curve for reliability; Typical margin: 20% below curve; Improves long-term reliability. Improving derating: Add airflow to extend full-power range; Use heatsink to reduce temperature rise; Select higher-power converter and operate at lower percentage. Multiple factors: Derating may also apply for: Low input voltage; High altitude; Reduced airflow. Check datasheet for specific curves for your product. Contact our FAE team for derating analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Operate below derating curve with margin. Consider airflow or heatsink to improve. Contact us for analysis.

faqs.question: What thermal interface material should I use?
faqs.answer: Thermal interface material (TIM) improves heat transfer: Types of TIM: Thermal grease: Best thermal performance; Messy application; Best for permanent installations. Thermal pads: Easy to apply; Good for production; Slightly higher thermal resistance. Phase change materials: Combine benefits of grease and pads; Good for high-volume production. Thermal adhesives: Permanent attachment; Good thermal conductivity; Electrical insulation. Selection considerations: Thermal resistance: Lower is better; Typical: 0.1-1.0°C-in²/W. Thickness: Thinner is better; Must accommodate surface flatness; Typical: 0.1-0.5mm. Electrical insulation: Required for electrically conductive packages; Not needed for isolated packages. Application guidelines: Clean surfaces before application; Apply thin, even layer; Eliminate air gaps; Ensure full contact area. Recommended products: Grease: Shin-Etsu X-23-7762, Dow Corning 340; Pads: Bergquist Gap Pad, Laird Tflex; Phase change: Honeywell PCM45F. For most Cincon applications, thermal pads offer good balance of performance and ease of use. Contact our FAE team for TIM recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Thermal pads good for production, grease for best performance. Contact us for recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I measure converter temperature in my application?
faqs.answer: Accurate temperature measurement ensures reliable operation: Measurement locations: Case temperature: Most practical measurement; Use thermocouple on case surface; Avoid air gaps between probe and case. Ambient temperature: Measure near converter inlet; Avoid heat sources and exhaust; Use multiple points for accuracy. Measurement techniques: Thermocouples: Type K most common; Small gauge wire (36 AWG) for accuracy; Secure with thermal epoxy or tape; Allow time to stabilize. Infrared thermometer: Non-contact measurement; Measure emissivity of surface; Can be affected by reflections; Good for quick checks. Thermal imaging: Visual temperature distribution; Identifies hot spots; Good for design validation; Professional equipment recommended. Best practices: Measure at worst-case conditions: Maximum load; Minimum airflow; Maximum ambient. Allow thermal stabilization: 15-30 minutes typical; Monitor until temperature stable. Document measurements: Record load, airflow, ambient; Note measurement location; Compare to datasheet limits. Safety margin: Keep 10-15°C below maximum rating; Improves long-term reliability; Accounts for measurement error. Contact our FAE team for temperature measurement guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Measure case temperature with thermocouple at worst-case conditions. Maintain margin below limits. Contact us for guidance.

customerCases.feedback: Guide was very helpful for our design

articles.title: Troubleshooting Cincon Converter Issues
articles.summary: Common issues and solutions when using Cincon DC-DC converters including startup problems, output issues, thermal concerns, and EMI challenges.
articles.content: This troubleshooting guide addresses common issues encountered when using Cincon converters. No Output Voltage: Check input power: Verify input voltage present at pins; Check polarity is correct; Measure input current (should be small at no load). Check enable pin: If converter has enable, verify it's in correct state; Some require pull-up or pull-down resistor. Check for faults: Short circuit on output triggers protection; Overload may cause shutdown; Allow time for auto-recovery. Output Voltage Low: Input voltage: Verify within specified range; Low input causes low output. Load current: Check if exceeding rated output; Overload causes voltage droop or shutdown. Line/load regulation: Some variation is normal; Check datasheet specifications. Thermal shutdown: Converter may be overheating; Check temperature and airflow. Excessive Ripple or Noise: Input capacitance: Verify adequate input capacitor; Place close to pins. Output capacitance: Check output capacitor value and ESR; Add capacitance if needed. Layout issues: Long traces increase inductance; Poor grounding causes noise. External interference: Nearby switching circuits; Poor shielding. Overheating: Power dissipation: Calculate expected dissipation; Verify within thermal limits. Airflow: Ensure adequate ventilation; Check for blocked airflow. Heatsinking: Verify proper heatsink installation; Check thermal interface material. Overloading: Verify load current within rating; Check for short circuits. Intermittent Operation: Loose connections: Check solder joints; Verify connector integrity. Thermal cycling: Expansion/contraction causes opens; Improve mechanical stability. Marginal design: Operating near limits; Add margin to design. EMI Issues: Layout: Minimize loop areas; Improve grounding. Filtering: Add input/output filters; Use shielded enclosures. Contact our FAE team for troubleshooting assistance.

faqs.question: My converter has no output. What should I check?
faqs.answer: No output can have several causes. Check in this order: Input power: Measure voltage at converter input pins (not just power supply); Verify polarity is correct; Check input fuse if present; Measure input current (should be mA at no load). Enable pin: Check if converter has enable pin; Verify enable voltage is correct (high or low depending on type); Add pull-up/pull-down resistor if needed; Check enable circuit for proper operation. Output conditions: Check for short circuit on output; Measure output resistance (should be high when off); Disconnect load and test converter alone. Protection status: Converter may be in protection mode; Short circuit protection latches or auto-recovers; Over-temperature protection requires cooling; Wait for auto-recovery or cycle power. Mechanical issues: Check solder joints for cold joints or cracks; Verify pins are properly seated; Check for damaged components. If still no output: Try known-good converter; Contact our FAE team for failure analysis; Provide input voltage, expected output, and measurements taken. Document all measurements for troubleshooting assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check input power, enable pin, output short, and protection status. Contact us if issue persists.

faqs.question: Why is my output voltage lower than expected?
faqs.answer: Low output voltage can be caused by several factors: Input voltage issues: Input below minimum specified voltage; Input voltage dropping under load; High resistance in input connections. Load conditions: Output overloaded (current > rating); Load has high inrush current; Short circuit or partial short on output. Line/load regulation: Some voltage variation is normal; Check datasheet for regulation specifications; Typically ±1-5% depending on product. Temperature effects: High temperature may cause derating; Check if converter is overheating; Verify thermal design. Wiring and connections: Voltage drop in long output wires; Insufficient wire gauge; Poor connections causing resistance. Measurement issues: Measure at converter pins (not at load); Use proper measurement technique; Account for probe loading. To diagnose: Measure input voltage at pins under load; Measure output current vs rating; Check converter temperature; Measure voltage directly at converter output pins. Contact our FAE team if voltage remains low after checking these items.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check input voltage, load current, and temperature. Measure at converter pins. Contact us for assistance.

faqs.question: How do I reduce output ripple and noise?
faqs.answer: Excessive ripple can be addressed through several methods: Output capacitance: Add more output capacitance; Use low-ESR ceramic capacitors; Place capacitors close to load; Typical: 10-100µF depending on requirements. Input capacitance: Ensure adequate input capacitor; Place close to converter pins; Reduces input ripple which affects output. Layout improvements: Minimize output loop area; Use ground planes; Keep traces short and wide; Separate noisy and sensitive traces. Filtering: Add LC filter on output; Use ferrite beads; Implement π-filter for severe cases. Load considerations: Some loads generate noise back to converter; Add load-side filtering; Use separate regulators for sensitive circuits. Measurement technique: Use proper oscilloscope probing; Short ground lead or use spring ground; Measure at converter output pins; Avoid picking up radiated noise. Typical ripple values: 50-100mV p-p for standard converters; 30-50mV with good filtering; <10mV possible with extensive filtering. For specific recommendations based on your ripple requirements, contact our FAE team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Add output capacitance, improve layout, add filtering. Contact us for specific recommendations.

faqs.question: My converter is getting too hot. What can I do?
faqs.answer: Overheating can be addressed through several approaches: Verify operating conditions: Measure actual load power; Check if exceeding rated output; Verify input voltage is correct; Measure ambient temperature. Improve thermal design: Add heatsink to converter; Improve airflow (add fan or increase ventilation); Reduce ambient temperature; Move away from heat sources. Reduce power dissipation: Use higher efficiency converter; Reduce load power if possible; Operate at lower input voltage if option available. Check for problems: Partial short causing excess current; Oscillation causing extra dissipation; Wrong converter for application. Heatsink options: Thermal pad to metal chassis; Clip-on heatsink for package; Custom heatsink for high power. Airflow improvements: Add system fan; Improve ventilation openings; Remove airflow obstructions; Consider forced convection. Derating: Reduce load power if possible; Use higher power rated converter; Accept reduced output at high temperature. If temperature remains excessive after these steps, contact our FAE team for thermal analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Add heatsink, improve airflow, or reduce load. Check for problems. Contact us for thermal analysis.

faqs.question: How do I fix EMI compliance issues?
faqs.answer: EMI issues can be addressed through systematic approaches: Conducted emissions (input): Add common mode choke on input; Add differential mode filter capacitor; Use shielded input cable; Improve input capacitor placement. Radiated emissions: Minimize loop areas (input and output); Use ground planes for shielding; Add shielding can over converter; Keep cables short and shielded. Layout improvements: Place input capacitor very close to pins; Minimize high-frequency loop areas; Use multilayer board with ground planes; Separate noisy and sensitive circuits. Filtering: Add π-filter on input and/or output; Use ferrite beads on all lines; Implement common mode filtering; Add Y-capacitors for common mode noise. Shielding: Metal enclosure for product; Converter shielding can; Shielded cables for I/O; Ground all shields properly. Testing and validation: Pre-compliance testing early in design; Identify worst-case operating modes; Test with final cables and enclosure; Allow margin for production variation. Standards compliance: Identify applicable standards (CISPR, FCC, etc.); Understand limits and frequency ranges; Plan for required margin (typically 6dB). Contact our FAE team for EMI design review and recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Add filtering, improve layout, use shielding. Test early and often. Contact us for design review.

customerCases.feedback: Guide was very helpful for our design
